startScreenOptions.title: AN1.1-AN1.2 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for AN1.1-AN1.2.

interactions.label: In the anatomical position, the palms face inward toward the thighs.

params.question: In the anatomical position, the palms face inward toward the thighs.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: In the anatomical position the palms face forward, not inward. The narration specifically flagged this as a common mistake. With palms forward, the radius and ulna are parallel, and directional terms like medial and lateral can be applied to the forearm without ambiguity.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. In the anatomical position the palms face forward (anteriorly), not inward. This detail distinguishes the anatomical position from a natural standing posture and ensures that terms like medial and lateral apply consistently to the forearm and hand.

interactions.label: A patient's wound is described as being on the medial aspect of the left arm. Th

params.question: A patient's wound is described as being on the medial aspect of the left arm. This means the wound is located:

answers.text: On the side of the arm facing away from the body

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: That would be the lateral aspect. Lateral means away from the midline — the opposite of medial.

answers.text: On the side of the arm closest to the trunk midline

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Medial means toward the midline of the body. On the left arm, the medial surface is the side that faces the trunk.

answers.text: On the front surface of the arm

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The front surface is the anterior (ventral) aspect, not the medial aspect. Medial refers to proximity to the midline, not front-to-back orientation.

answers.text: On the back surface of the arm

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The back surface is the posterior (dorsal) aspect. Medial and lateral describe the side-to-side axis relative to the midline, not the front-to-back axis.

overallFeedback.feedback: Medial means toward the midline of the body. On either arm, the medial surface is the side that faces the trunk. Review the directional pairs: medial/lateral describe the side-to-side axis, while anterior/posterior describe the front-to-back axis.
Well done. Medial always means toward the midline, regardless of which limb or side of the body is being described.

interactions.label: A physiotherapist asks a patient to move their arm away from the body's midline

params.question: A physiotherapist asks a patient to move their arm away from the body's midline in the coronal plane. Which movement is being described?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Flexion decreases the angle at a joint and occurs in the sagittal plane, not the coronal plane.
Adduction does occur in the coronal plane, but it moves the limb toward the midline, not away from it. You may be confusing it with abduction.
Abduction moves a limb away from the midline and occurs in the coronal (frontal) plane. The memory aid: abduction takes you absent from the midline.
Medial rotation turns the limb toward the midline around its long axis and occurs in the transverse plane, not the coronal plane.

overallFeedback.feedback: The movement away from the midline in the coronal plane is abduction. Remember: abduction = absent from midline, adduction = adding back. Flexion/extension occur in the sagittal plane, and rotation occurs in the transverse plane.
Correct. Abduction moves a limb away from the midline in the coronal plane. Its opposite, adduction, brings the limb back toward the midline in the same plane.

interactions.label: In adults, red bone marrow is found primarily in the medullary cavity of long bo

params.question: In adults, red bone marrow is found primarily in the medullary cavity of long bones.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is false. The medullary cavity of long bones contains yellow marrow, not red. In adults, red marrow is located in the spongy bone of flat bones (sternum, ilium) and the epiphyses of long bones. Think of it this way: the medullary cavity is the fuel storage tank (yellow/fat), while the spongy regions are the production floor (red/blood cells).
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. In adults, the medullary cavity of long bones contains yellow marrow (adipocytes). Red marrow is found in the spongy bone of flat bones such as the sternum and ilium, and in the epiphyses of long bones, where haematopoiesis actively occurs.

interactions.label: Approximately what percentage of bone's dry weight is made up of the inorganic m

params.question: Approximately what percentage of bone's dry weight is made up of the inorganic mineral component (hydroxyapatite)?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: 35% is the proportion of the organic component (mainly collagen), not the inorganic mineral. The two components add up to 100% of dry weight.
The split is not equal. The inorganic mineral component predominates, making bone hard and compression-resistant, while the smaller organic fraction provides flexibility.
Hydroxyapatite makes up approximately 65% of bone's dry weight, providing hardness and compressive strength — like the concrete in reinforced concrete.
80% would overestimate the mineral content. If bone were 80% mineral, it would lack sufficient collagen for flexibility and would fracture too easily under bending forces.

overallFeedback.feedback: Bone's dry weight is approximately 65% inorganic (hydroxyapatite) and 35% organic (collagen). Remember the reinforced-concrete analogy: 65% concrete (mineral for hardness) and 35% steel rebar (collagen for flexibility).
Correct. The 65% inorganic (hydroxyapatite) to 35% organic (collagen) ratio gives bone its unique combination of hardness and flexibility.
